solver_view.stem: A 60 Ah prismatic NCM/graphite cell undergoes thermal runaway in an EV-ARC test. An internal thermocouple records a maximum temperature of 994.8 °C and a peak rate of 531 °C/s. A surface thermocouple on the same cell records simultaneously. Which statements about the surface thermocouple readings are supported by engineering evidence?
options: A. The surface thermocouple maximum temperature (~565.9 °C) underestimates the internal peak by roughly 430 °C, so surface measurements alone cannot characterize peak internal severity.

B. The surface temperature variation rate (~49.2 °C/s) is approximately one order of magnitude lower than the internal rate, meaning surface-based rate-of-rise alarms will underestimate internal escalation speed.
C. Surface and internal thermocouples reach the same maximum temperature because thermal equilibrium is established before the peak is recorded.
D. The surface thermocouple provides an earlier onset warning than the internal thermocouple because surface sensors respond to convective heat before internal reactions begin.
E. The gap between surface and internal peak temperatures implies that using surface temperature as a proxy for internal state leads to a conservative (safe-side) overestimate of internal severity.
F. Voltage drop to near zero occurs 6.8 s before thermal runaway onset and therefore provides an earlier electrical warning than either thermocouple measurement of the runaway event itself.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Surface max 565.9 °C vs internal 994.8 °C; difference ~429 °C confirms surface underestimates internal peak severity.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Surface rate max 49.2 °C/s vs internal 531 °C/s; one-order-of-magnitude gap confirmed, rate alarms underestimate escalation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows a ~430 °C gap at peak; thermal equilibrium is not established, making this a boundary overreach.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence supports surface sensors giving earlier onset warning; onset temperature T1 is defined from the internal thermocouple.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Surface reads lower, not higher, than internal; using surface as proxy is non-conservative, not conservative.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Voltage drops 6.8 s before TR onset, predating the thermal runaway temperature rise detected by either thermocouple.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Measurement-versus-internal-state gap: surface thermocouple underestimates internal peak temperature and rate; voltage provides earlier warning than thermal sensors.
analysis.primary_boundary: Findings apply to the specific 60 Ah prismatic NCM/graphite cell under EV-ARC adiabatic conditions; equilibrium assumption is explicitly violated.
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming surface and internal temperatures equalize before peak
Assuming surface sensors provide earlier onset detection than internal sensors
Treating surface underestimate as conservative overestimate

solver_view.stem: An engineer compares LFP and NCM battery chemistries under extreme adiabatic conditions and notes that the ratio of triggering energy of self-heat onset to total self-heat generation is ~24.5% for LFP and ~5–8% for NCM. The engineer then designs a thermal management intervention. Which conclusions about these chemistries and intervention design are valid?
options: A. For NCM batteries under adiabatic conditions, internal chemical reactions account for approximately 95% of heat sources during thermal runaway, leaving external input as a minor contributor.

B. Because LFP requires ~24.5% external triggering energy relative to total self-heat, LFP batteries demand substantially more external energy input to initiate thermal runaway than NCM batteries under comparable adiabatic conditions.
C. The ~5–8% onset ratio for NCM means that once NCM self-heating begins, the remaining ~92–95% of heat is released rapidly, implying less intervention time than for LFP after onset.
D. The consistent onset-to-total-heat ratio across LFP cells (mean 24.5%, median 23.1%) means this ratio can be directly applied to predict NCM thermal runaway onset energy without adjustment.

E. For LFP, approximately 65.5% of total self-heat is released during the thermal runaway stage itself, meaning most LFP self-heat is concentrated in a short burst after the triggering threshold is crossed.

F. Under adiabatic conditions, LFP batteries derive roughly 80% of total energy from internal exothermic reactions, so external heating accounts for the minority of total energy even though it may exceed the triggering threshold.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states internal chemical interactions account for ~95% of NCM heat sources under extreme adiabatic conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The ratio is onset-to-total-self-heat, not an absolute energy comparison between chemistries; absolute triggering energy comparison is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Low onset ratio (~5–8%) for NCM means ~92–95% of heat remains post-onset, implying a larger and faster self-sustaining release with less intervention time.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly shows NCM ratio (~5–8%) differs substantially from LFP (~24.5%); direct transfer across chemistries is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states ~65.5% of LFP self-heat is released during thermal runaway in a short period, causing steep temperature rise.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states LFP gets ~80% of energy from internal reactions and ~20% from external input under adiabatic conditions.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Chemistry-specific onset-to-total-heat ratios and their implications for intervention timing and energy partitioning.
analysis.primary_boundary: Ratios are defined under extreme adiabatic conditions; direct cross-chemistry transfer of LFP ratios to NCM is explicitly unsupported.
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying LFP onset ratio directly to NCM
Comparing absolute triggering energies across chemistries from ratio data alone

solver_view.stem: A battery pack engineer evaluates thermal runaway propagation in a multi-cell prismatic pack. Cells are arranged in series with silicon rubber pads between battery sets. Under uphill climbing at 120 km/h, 3% slope, 40 °C ambient, thermal runaway is triggered. Which statements about propagation dynamics and early warning are supported?
options: A. The thermal runaway propagation time interval across a silicon rubber pad (~120 s) is approximately twice that between adjacent cells within the same set (~60 s), indicating the pad provides meaningful but not absolute propagation delay.
B. Approximately 47% of total cell energy during thermal runaway is ejected by venting, making venting the largest single energy loss pathway from the triggering cell.

C. Heat conduction dominates energy transfer before thermal runaway onset; after onset, reaction heat becomes the dominant pathway, so pre-runaway thermal monitoring primarily captures conductive heat flow.

D. Uphill climbing at 120 km/h, 3% slope, 40 °C ambient produces the shortest propagation time intervals among studied conditions because it yields the highest initial battery temperature at runaway onset.
E. Lateral surface temperature monitoring reliably detects thermal runaway in adjacent cells within seconds because propagation between neighboring cells takes only ~60 s.
F. Nail penetration as an abuse trigger introduces a unique fire hazard not present in non-penetration tests because air ingress through the penetration hole can ignite vented electrolyte.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states silicon rubber pad interval ~120 s vs adjacent cell interval ~60 s; ratio of ~2× is directly supported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states ~47% of total energy ejected by venting, exceeding the ~41% used to heat the cell itself.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states heat conduction dominates before runaway; reaction heat dominates after onset in the propagating cell.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Uphill climbing yields highest initial temperature at onset (47.4 °C) and shortest propagation intervals among the four studied conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence warns lateral temperature detection can be delayed during localized heating; 78 s propagation interval means surface monitoring may miss early onset.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states air ingress from nail hole can cause fire on electrolyte during venting, a hazard absent without air.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Propagation dynamics, energy partitioning, dominant heat transfer pathway shift, and abuse-method-specific hazards in multi-cell packs.
analysis.primary_boundary: Propagation intervals and energy fractions are specific to the tested pack geometry and conditions; lateral temperature monitoring limitation applies to localized heating scenarios.
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming lateral surface monitoring provides reliable real-time detection within the ~60 s propagation window
Generalizing uphill climbing result to all operating conditions unconditionally

solver_view.prompt: A battery management system designer is evaluating three early warning strategies for lithium-ion battery thermal runaway: (1) surface temperature monitoring, (2) voltage monitoring, and (3) acoustic/sound-based monitoring. For each strategy, identify one confirmed limitation and one confirmed advantage based on engineering evidence. Then explain why a sound-based warning system may not be directly applicable to flexible pouch (soft-case) battery packs, even if it performs well on square hard-case batteries.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Surface temperature monitoring: advantage—widely deployable; limitation—surface readings lag and underestimate internal temperatures (surface max ~565.9 °C vs internal ~994.8 °C) and surface rate of rise is ~10× lower than internal rate, so it cannot characterize internal severity accurately. Temperature and voltage-based warnings can only be realized 3–5 minutes in advance when the battery is already at the runaway boundary. Voltage monitoring: advantage—voltage drop (e.g., 3.22 V to 0.25 V within 1 s) occurs 6.8 s before thermal runaway onset, providing an earlier electrical warning than thermal sensors for the runaway event itself; limitation—voltage cannot localize internal events or detect gradual degradation reliably. Acoustic monitoring: advantage—sound-based warning can be issued approximately 7000 s earlier than temperature or stress-based methods on average; limitation—the safety valve release sound of flexible pouch (soft-case) batteries is very weak and cannot realize thermal runaway warning, because the soft casing absorbs pressure waves from gas expansion rather than concentrating them as a rigid hard-case does. Therefore, acoustic warning systems validated on square hard-case batteries cannot be directly transferred to soft-case packs without redesign.
must_include: Surface temperature underestimates internal peak temperature
Temperature/voltage warning only 3–5 minutes before runaway boundary
Voltage drop occurs 6.8 s before thermal runaway onset
Acoustic warning approximately 7000 s earlier than temperature-based methods
Soft-case battery safety valve sound is too weak for acoustic warning
Soft casing absorbs pressure waves, reducing acoustic signal intensity
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming acoustic warning works equally well for all battery formats without format qualification
Claiming voltage monitoring provides warning earlier than acoustic monitoring in general
Claiming surface temperature monitoring is conservative (safe-side) relative to internal state
Applying the 7000 s acoustic advantage figure to soft-case batteries

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Comparative early warning strategy evaluation: measurement-versus-internal-state gap for thermal sensors, temporal advantage of voltage and acoustic signals, and format-dependent limitation of acoustic detection.
analysis.primary_boundary: Acoustic advantage (~7000 s) and sound-based warning are established only for square hard-case batteries; soft-case limitation is explicitly stated. Voltage drop timing (6.8 s) is specific to the tested prismatic NCM/graphite cell.
intended_error_modes: Regime transfer error: applying acoustic warning performance from hard-case to soft-case batteries
Causal overclaim: asserting surface temperature provides conservative overestimate of internal severity
Boundary overreach: claiming voltage warning is generally earlier than acoustic warning across all conditions
